narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we could support—it's not about your worth, but a story woven from the patterns in your profile. At the top of what stood out was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, the strongest signal pulling toward potential failure, as it placed you at the bottom of your school's rankings that year, suggesting challenges in sustaining strong performance as law school demands ramp up. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 1.0, reinforcing a pattern of early struggles that the model saw as a key hurdle for bar success. Your LSAT score of 25.0 added to this picture, landing lower than what typically lights the way for passers. Even with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.5, your middle family income bracket entered the story as a moderate influence toward difficulty, perhaps hinting at fewer resources for the intense prep ahead. Your Asian background and female gender played supporting roles in this narrative, aligning with trends the model has learned from past students facing similar odds. Choosing full-time study rounded out the influences, as it often means juggling more without the flexibility part-timers sometimes have. Together, these threads painted a 64.9% chance of bar failure in the model's eyes—a mosaic of early academic deciles, test scores, and life factors that, sadly, tipped the scales against your application. But here's the empowering part: this isn't destiny; your 3.5 UGPA shows real grit, and targeted steps like LSAT retakes or study habit shifts could rewrite your story for another school. You've got the drive—keep pushing forward. With encouragement, [Your Guide]
